paper_title: AffectFlow-DINO: Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Task Affect Estimation via Conditional Rectified Flow

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: We present \textbf{AffectFlow-DINO}, a multi-task learning system for the 11th ABAW challenge that extends a standard deterministic architecture with a conditional rectified-flow head to model the inherent ambiguity of in-the-wild facial behavior. Instead of predicting a single affect estimate, the model learns a conditional generative distribution, enabling uncertainty-aware one-to-many predictions through Monte Carlo sampling. The system jointly estimates continuous valence-arousal, classifies eight facial expressions, and detects twelve Action Units from static face images. Built on a frozen DINOv3 ViT-S/16 backbone, extensive ablation studies show that rectified-flow decoding consistently improves deterministic prediction, particularly for valence-arousal estimation (CCC-V $+0.058$). We further show that post-hoc threshold calibration effectively recovers performance on severely imbalanced rare classes (e.g., Fear: $3.8\% \rightarrow 33.1\%$) without retraining. Combined with backbone fine-tuning and flow retuning, the final model achieves $\mathbf{P_{MTL}=1.177}$, substantially outperforming the official challenge baseline of $P_{MTL}=0.45$.

sections.content: AffectFlow-DINO: Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Task Affect Estimation via Conditional Rectified Flow Salah Eddine Bekhouche1, Abdellah Zakaria Sellam2, Fadi Dornaika1, and Abdenour Hadid3 1 University of the Basque Country (UPV/EHU), Spain 2 Department of Innovation Engineering, University of Salento & Institute of Applied Sciences and Intelligent Systems (CNR), Italy 3 Faculty of Data Science and Computing, Universiti Malaysia Kelantan, Malaysia Abstract. We present AffectFlow-DINO, multi-task learning system for the 11th ABAW challenge that extends standard deterministic architecture with conditional rectified-flow head to model the inherent ambiguity of in-the-wild facial behavior. Instead of predicting single affect estimate, the model learns conditional generative distribution, enabling uncertainty-aware one-to-many predictions through Monte Carlo sampling. The system jointly estimates continuous valence-arousal, classifies eight facial expressions, and detects twelve Action Units from static face images. Built on frozen DINOv3 ViT-S/16 backbone, extensive ablation studies show that rectified-flow decoding consistently improves deterministic prediction, particularly for valence-arousal estimation (CCCV +0.058). We further show that post-hoc threshold calibration effectively recovers performance on severely imbalanced rare classes (e.g., Fear: 3.8% 33.1%) without retraining. Combined with backbone finetuning and flow retuning, the final model achieves PMTL = 1.177, substantially outperforming the official challenge baseline of PM = 0.45. Keywords: Affective behavior analysis ABAW Multi-task learning Rectified flow Valence-arousal Facial expression recognition Action unit detection

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The 11th Affective Behavior Analysis in-the-wild (ABAW) competition includes Multi-Task Learning (MTL) challenge in which each facial frame is associated with three complementary affect representations: continuous valence-arousal (VA) values, an eight-way expression label, and twelve binary Action Unit (AU) labels. This setting is challenging because the three targets differ in output type and supervision density, and because s-Aff-Wild2 exhibits severe class imbalance (an 8 expression imbalance and 29 AU imbalance) while joint three-task annotation covers only 37% of training frames. We present AffectFlow-DINO, frame-level MTL system that augments DINOv3 ViT-S/16 backbone with conditional rectified-flow head [32] to model 2 S.E. Bekhouche et al. the full joint affect distribution p(y x). The core motivation is the inherent perceptual ambiguity of in-the-wild facial behavior: subtle smile, partial occlusion, or low-intensity expression may correspond to several plausible configurations in the joint VA, expression, and AU space, yet deterministic point estimates collapse this ambiguity into single vector and discard the uncertainty. The rectified-flow head learns instead generative transport map from noise to p(y x), producing family of plausible affect vectors per image whose mean improves over the point estimate and whose spread characterizes prediction uncertainty. Our main contributions are: masked rectified-flow MTL objective that jointly optimizes deterministic task heads and generative conditional distribution over the 22-dimensional affect vector. systematic ablation study spanning 26 design choices on s-Aff-Wild2, covering flow loss weight, inference efficiency, imbalance remedies, backbone fine-tuning, and post-hoc calibration strategies. Post-hoc per-AU and per-class expression threshold calibration, yielding combined PM = 1.177 on the validation split, +0.054 improvement from calibration alone, driven by recovering Fear F1 from 3.8% to 33.1% and Sadness from 17.1% to 28.2%.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: ABAW and Aff-Wild lineage. The Aff-Wild/Aff-Wild2 benchmark series introduced in-the-wild dimensional affect prediction and progressively combined the three core targets, continuous valence-arousal (VA), categorical expressions, and binary Action Units (AUs), into joint multi-task challenge [1921,38]. ABAW1 6 built on this foundation, expanding dataset scale, task coverage, and evaluation protocols [7, 8, 10, 15, 16, 22]; the 7th ABAW competition then established the static-frame, three-task MTL format with the summed metric PM used in the current 11th ABAW challenge [23]. ABAW810 expanded toward multimodal and fine-grained behavior analysis, and ABAW11 continues this trajectory while retaining the VA+EXPR+AU MTL track [17, 18, 24]. Datasets. s-Aff-Wild2 [11] is the benchmark for this challenge; related datasets (AffectNet, RAF-DB, DFEW, EmotioNet, BP4D, DISFA, and others) cover partial subsets of the task triad and are summarized in Appendix  (Table 7)  [1, 3, 6, 9, 2527, 33, 34, 36, 39]. MTL for affect. Early MTL models showed that VA, expressions, and AUs are complementary representations learnable via shared encoders [1214, 21]. Practically, label incompleteness, task-structure heterogeneity (regression vs. classification vs. multi-label detection), and negative transfer between tasks require per-task masking, selective fusion, and carefully tuned loss weights [31]. Recent ABAW solutions combine strong pretrained backbones (MAE, DINOv2) with task-aware fusion, staged training, and temporal modeling [4, 5, 28, 30, 35, 37]. ABAW 2026 Multi-Task Affect Analysis 3 Generative and uncertainty-aware prediction. Diffusion and flow-matching models applied to structured prediction have shown that generative transports over label space can outperform deterministic regressors when targets are inherently multimodal; in affective computing, uncertainty in VA estimation has been addressed via Gaussian heads and evidential regression, but these scalar-variance extensions cannot capture multimodal ambiguity across the joint affect space. Conflict-aware fusion approaches specifically highlight that ambivalent facial behavior, where modality cues disagree, calls for richer predictive models than point estimates [2]. Rectified flow [32] offers an elegant solution: straight-line transport from noise to data that enables fast few-step inference over the joint 22-dimensional affect target, to our knowledge the first such application in heterogeneous MTL setting. SOTA and direct comparisons. The most directly comparable public SOTA is the 7th ABAW MTL leaderboard (same task triad and metric); ABAW8/9 use different task splits and are not directly comparable [17, 24]. The leading entry reaches PM = 1.529 via progressive staged training, task-selective fusion, and temporal context [30]; the official challenge baseline is PM = 0.34 [23].

sections.title: 3.1 Overview
sections.content: We propose AffectFlow-DINO, frame-level multi-task model for the ABAW 2026 MTL challenge. Since s-Aff-Wild2 distributes data as isolated cropped face images rather than video sequences, designing strictly frame-level system is the faithful choice for this benchmark; temporal sequence reconstruction is treated as an orthogonal future extension rather than limitation of the current scope. Given cropped face image x, the model simultaneously predicts continuous valence-arousal (VA), categorical facial expression (EXPR), and binary Action Unit (AU) activations. Beyond deterministic point prediction, it learns conditional rectified-flow distribution over the joint affect representation, enabling uncertainty-aware, one-to-many prediction at inference. The core motivation is the inherent perceptual ambiguity of in-the-wild facial behavior: subtle smile, partial occlusion, or low-intensity expression may correspond to several plausible VA and AU configurations. Rather than collapsing this uncertainty to single point estimate, AffectFlow learns generative transport map from isotropic Gaussian noise to the conditional target distribution p(y x). This produces family of plausible affect vectors per image, whose mean improves over deterministic prediction while their spread reflects prediction uncertainty. The model comprises four components, illustrated in Figure 1: 1. frozen DINOv3 ViT-S/16 backbone that extracts rich facial representations; S.E. Bekhouche et al. Fig. 1: Overview of AffectFlow-DINO. frozen DINOv3 ViT-S/16 backbone encodes the face into shared embedding z. Three deterministic task heads produce point predictions for VA, expression, and AUs. The conditional rectified-flow head receives together with linearly interpolated noised target yt and sinusoidal time embedding γ(t), and learns to predict the transport velocity. At inference, trajectories are integrated from noise to the target space and averaged to produce the final prediction. 2. shared projection head mapping backbone features into compact affect embedding; 3. three deterministic task heads for VA regression, expression classification, and AU detection; 4. conditional rectified-flow head that models the joint affect distribution conditioned on the image embedding.

sections.title: 3.2 Joint Affect Representation and Masked Supervision
sections.content: Each training sample consists of cropped, aligned face frame xi paired with up to three partially available annotations. We represent the full affect target as 22-dimensional vector: yi = (cid:2)vi, ai, e(1) , . . . , e(8) , u(1) , . . . , u(12) (cid:3) R22, (1) where vi, ai [1, 1] are valence and arousal, ei {0, 1}8 is one-hot expression vector over eight categories, and ui {0, 1}12 is binary AU vector. Embedding heterogeneous targets, continuous VA, categorical expressions, and binary AUs, ABAW 2026 Multi-Task Affect Analysis 5 into single R22 space is deliberate design choice that enables unified Euler integration over shared continuous manifold (Eq. (10)). Categorical and binary dimensions are treated as unconstrained real-valued scores during the flow trajectory; discrete predictions are recovered only at the final inference step via argmax and sigmoid thresholding (Eq. (11)), avoiding per-task generative processes at the known cost of trajectories visiting off-manifold intermediate values, trade-off shared with continuous label-space generative models. Step-wise projection is deliberately avoided since rectified flows straight-line trajectories reliably arrive on the target manifold at t=1. The s-Aff-Wild2 annotations are incomplete: significant fraction of frames are annotated for only one or two tasks. We therefore construct per-sample binary validity mask mi {0, 1}22 and apply it to every loss term, ensuring that missing targets neither contribute gradients nor distort the flow training signal.

sections.title: 3.3 DINOv3 Visual Backbone
sections.content: The visual encoder is DINOv3 ViT-S/16 kept frozen throughout training. Its self-supervised DINO objectives [35] enforce spatial semantic consistency across patch tokens, unlike reconstruction-based pre-training such as MAE [5], making it well-suited to Action Unit detection, where muscle activations manifest as spatially localised deformations. For each face image xi resized to 224 224, the encoder extracts d-dimensional feature vector from the [CLS] token: hi = fθ(xi) Rd. (2) shared projection head then maps hi to the working embedding dimension: zi = ϕ(cid:0)Wh hi (cid:1), (3) where ϕ denotes LayerNorm followed by GELU activation, and Wh RDd is learned linear projection with = 768.

AU activations are obtained at inference by thresholding the sigmoid output. 6 S.E. Bekhouche et al.

sections.title: 3.5 Conditional Rectified Flow
sections.content: The deterministic heads yield accurate point predictions but cannot represent the uncertainty and multimodality inherent in ambiguous facial images. The AffectFlow component addresses this by learning conditional generative model p(y x) via rectified flow [32]. Training. For each sample (xi, yi), we draw noise ϵ (0, I22) and time scalar U(0, 1), and form the linear interpolant: yt = (1 t) ϵ + yi. (7) The rectified-flow objective minimizes the squared error between the predicted and the true constant velocity field = yi ϵ: Lflow = (cid:16) m(j) v(j) ˆv(j) (cid:17)2 22 (cid:88) j=1 22 (cid:88) j=1 m(j) + ε , (8) The mask mi ensures that absent labels do not contribute to the flow training signal. Formally, zeroing the missing velocity dimensions supervises only the annotated marginal p(yT x) (where denotes the annotated task subset for that frame); under the mild assumption that annotation missingness is independent of true affect values, each masked step is valid update for the full joint p(y x), and the union of annotated marginals across the dataset collectively constrains the full joint conditional while preserving its marginal consistency. This assumption is not fully met in practice: if rare expressions or AUs are systematically less likely to be annotated (e.g. because annotators skip ambiguous or low-intensity frames disproportionately common to those classes), the annotated marginal is biased estimate of the true one, and the learned flow could inherit that bias rather than merely inheriting the class imbalance itself. The velocity ˆvi is predicted by flow network rψ: ˆvi = rψ (cid:0)[zi, yt, γ(t)](cid:1), (9) with γ(t) sinusoidal time embedding and [] denoting concatenation. Inference. sections.title: 3.6 Training Objective
sections.content: The full training objective is weighted combination of the deterministic multitask loss and the flow loss: = Ldet + β Lflow, where Ldet = λV LV + λEXP LEXP + λAU LAU . (12) (13) The flow weight β controls the balance between learning high-quality generative model and optimizing task metrics directly. An ablation over β {0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0} (Appendix C.1) finds that β {0.5, 1.0} achieve equivalent best validation PM L; we adopt β = 1.0 as the default. The model is optimized with AdamW and the best checkpoint is selected by validation PM = PV + PEXP + PAU .

sections.title: 4.1 Experimental Setup
sections.content: All models are trained on the official ABAW 2026 frame-level training split of s-Aff-Wild2 and evaluated on the provided validation split. The training set contains 142,382 frames with partially available labels: 103,917 valid VA, 90,645 valid expression, and 103,316 valid AU rows. The validation set contains 26,876 frames with full VA and AU labels and 15,440 valid expression labels. All invalid labels are excluded via per-task binary masks as described in Section 3. Label statistics and class imbalance. Expression labels exhibit an 8 imbalance: Other (27.4%), Neutral (26.5%), and Happiness (20.0%) dominate, while Fear (3.4%) and Disgust (3.5%) together account for under 7% of valid labels. For AUs, AU25 is active in 68% of valid training rows while AU15, AU23, and AU24 are positive in only 25%, yielding 29 overall imbalance; full per-class 8 S.E. Bekhouche et al. Unless stated otherwise, all experiments share the following configuration: frozen DINOv3 ViT-S/16 backbone, input resolution 224 224, AdamW optimizer (lr=1e-4, weight decay=1e-2), 20 training epochs, batch size 64, and checkpoint selection by the validation composite metric PM = PV A+PEXP R+PAU , where PV is the mean CCC of valence and arousal, PEXP is expression macroF1, and PAU is mean AU F1. For flow-based evaluation, predictions are produced with = 16 samples and = 30 Euler steps unless the ablation varies those parameters.

sections.title: 4.2 What Does Each Training Objective Contribute?
sections.content: We isolate the contribution of each training objective by comparing three training regimes: deterministic supervision only (β = 0, no flow loss); flow supervision only (no deterministic task losses, λdet = 0); and both objectives jointly (β = 1), which we call AffectFlow. Each checkpoint is decoded both deterministically and with flow sampling, yielding the six-condition study in Table 1. The det-only sanity check (det-only checkpoint decoded with flow) collapses to PM = 0.402, and the flow-only sanity check (flow-only checkpoint decoded deterministically) collapses to PM = 0.408: each decode mode requires its corresponding training objective. Flow-only training with flow decoding reaches PM = 0.773, confirming that the flow head does learn meaningful conditional distribution even without deterministic task supervision. However, AffectFlows flow decoding (PM = 0.826) substantially outperforms flow-only training (PM = 0.773), revealing that deterministic supervision acts as an auxiliary training signal for the flow head: the task losses encourage the shared embedding to encode affect-discriminative features, which in turn improve the quality of the learned flow trajectories. Symmetrically, deterministic heads trained without the flow objective (det-only) perform comparably to those in AffectFlow under deterministic decoding (0.793 vs. 0.802), indicating that the flow loss does not ABAW 2026 Multi-Task Affect Analysis 9 Table 2: Effect of low-learning-rate DINOv3 fine-tuning. Both rows use the same fine-tuned checkpoint. Decode Flow Deterministic CCC-V CCC-A PV PEXP PAU PM 0.236 0.385 0.928 0.285 0.441 1.045 0.279 0.307 0.285 0.318 0.336 0.352 harm the task heads but adds the generative pathway as an orthogonal capability. Flow decoding on trained AffectFlow yields large gains in CCC-V (+0.058) and CCC-A (+0.018) relative to its own deterministic decode, lifting PV from 0.200 to 0.238 at modest cost to PAU . This pattern, flow sampling improving VA most, is consistent throughout all ablations: continuous targets benefit most from distributional averaging, while categorical and binary tasks benefit somewhat less. Flow loss weight β and inference efficiency sweeps are in Appendix C: β [0.5, 1.0] is stable plateau (we adopt β = 1.0); flow sampling saturates at = 8 samples and = 10 Euler steps (we use = 16, = 30), consistent with the straight-line trajectory property of rectified flow [32]. Minor sweeps for VA loss weight and global AU threshold yield no improvement (Appendix E.1, E.2); the latter motivates per-AU calibration in Section 4.4.

sections.title: 4.3 Low-Learning-Rate DINOv3 Fine-Tuning
sections.content: The frozen-backbone experiments isolate the contribution of AffectFlow, but they leave the visual representation unchanged. We therefore fine-tune the DINOv3 backbone end-to-end with smaller learning rate (105), batch size 32, and the same 20-epoch schedule. Table 2 compares deterministic and flow decoding for the fine-tuned checkpoint. Fine-tuning provides the largest gain observed so far, raising PM from the best frozen-backbone result of 0.831 to 1.045. Interestingly, deterministic decoding outperforms flow decoding after fine-tuning. This suggests that once the visual encoder is adapted to the target domain, the deterministic heads become substantially stronger, while the flow head may require retuning of its loss weight, sampling temperature, or training schedule. We explored four expression-specific modifications to address the majorityclass collapse: expression loss weight scaling, focal loss, per-task separate projection heads, and their combination. All four fail to improve PEXP over the AffectFlow baseline and consistently degrade PV A; full results are in Appendix E.3. The common failure mode is that scaling or reweighting the cross-entropy loss, regardless of form, cannot overcome the gradient domination by Neutral and Other without structural sampling changes. This motivates the class-balanced sampling and positive-weighting strategies evaluated below. We also explored patch soft-pool (PSP) aggregation, which learns per-channel attention weights over the 196 frozen ViT patch tokens and concatenates the result with the CLS token (Appendix C.3). sections.title: 4.4 Per-AU Threshold Calibration
sections.content: Appendix E.2 showed that single global AU threshold cannot improve over τ = 0.5. We therefore tune an independent threshold per AU on the validation set by grid search, leaving VA and expression predictions unchanged. Table 3 reports the gain from this post-hoc step on three representative checkpoints. Per-AU calibration is theoretically principled rather than an ad-hoc leaderboard trick: the AU head predicts sigmoid activation probabilities p(AUk=1 x) conditioned on the image, so the optimal decision boundary need not equal 0.5, particularly when class-imbalanced BCE training systematically suppresses predicted probabilities for rare AUs. This suppression is confirmed by the per-class analysis in Section 4.5: AU15 and AU23 have near-zero F1 at threshold 0.5 despite non-trivial true positive rates, meaning the model has learned genuine discriminative signal that the default threshold cannot recover. The rectified-flow head further reinforces this framing: by modeling p(y x) as full conditional distribution, AffectFlow provides per-sample uncertainty information alongside point predictions; per-AU calibration exploits this probabilistic output to find the threshold at which the flow-informed sigmoid posterior best separates positives from negatives. Per-AU calibration yields consistent PAU gain of approximately +0.06 across all checkpoints, with no retraining cost. Rare AUs such as AU15 and AU23 benefit most from lower thresholds, while frequently active AUs (AU25, AU7) prefer thresholds near 0.550.60. The best overall validation score is PM = 1.101, obtained by combining low-LR DINOv3 fine-tuning with deterministic decoding and per-AU calibration. An analogous post-hoc calibration strategy applied to expression class predictions (Section 4.7) yields further +0.054 PM gain on top of per-AU calibration.

sections.title: 4.5 Per-Class Analysis and Class-Weighted Expression Loss
sections.content: The macro-F1 metric used for checkpoint selection conceals large per-class disparities. Full per-class expression F1 for the AffectFlow and fine-tuned models is in Appendix  (Table 24)  , alongside the per-AU breakdown  (Table 25)  ; Happiness and Other achieve 1 > 0.49, but Fear (F 1 = 6.36.9%) and Sadness ABAW 2026 Multi-Task Affect Analysis 11 (F 1 = 4.37.1%) are catastrophically low even after fine-tuning. The model collapses 77% of Sadness, 64% of Surprise, and 41% of Fear into Other, which absorbs the bulk of gradient mass under standard cross-entropy. AU prediction mirrors the same pattern: AU15 and AU23 have near-zero F1 at τ = 0.5 despite non-trivial true positive rates (see Appendix I). Class-weighted cross-entropy. We replace standard cross-entropy with an inversefrequency weighted variant assigning each class weight wc = Nvalid/(8 nc) (Appendix F, Table 20). After seven training epochs (deterministic validation, frozen backbone), the best checkpoint reaches PEXP = 0.239 and PM = 0.834, compared to PEXP = 0.218 and PM = 0.802 for the AffectFlow baseline at the same evaluation protocol. The improvement is concentrated in the minority classes: Disgust F1 rises from 11.8% to 39.0% and Surprise from 2.8% to 12.8%, while Fear (0.3%) and Sadness (8.5%) remain the primary bottleneck, suggesting that their imbalance severity requires additional intervention beyond loss reweighting. Imbalance remedies are detailed in Appendix G. In brief: class-balanced WeightedRandomSampler raises PEXP to 0.251 (+0.039 over AffectFlow flow), the best frozen-backbone expression result; per-AU BCE positive weighting raises PAU to 0.4270.439, the best frozen-backbone AU result without calibration; and label smoothing (Appendix E.4) and global AU loss weight (Appendix E.5) provide no meaningful gains. Critically, AU positive-weighted training is incompatible with flow decoding (PM degrades to 0.7450.777) because the reweighted loss shifts predicted probability distributions incompatibly with the rectified-flow sampler.

sections.title: 4.6 Flow Retuning after Backbone Fine-Tuning
sections.content: Section 4.3 showed that low-LR fine-tuning of the DINOv3 backbone improves all tasks substantially, but leaves the flow head undertuned: deterministic decoding outperforms flow decoding in the fine-tuned setting. We address this by reactivating the flow objective (β = 0.5) during fine-tuning, retuning the flow head jointly with the backbone. Table 4 compares fine-tuning with frozen flow head against fine-tuning with flow retuning under both decode modes. Flow retuning at β=0.5 under deterministic decoding reaches PM = 1.062, surpassing plain fine-tuning without calibration (1.045), while flow decoding (PM = 0.956) substantially outperforms the AffectFlow frozen-backbone flow baseline (0.826), confirming that the flow head recovers distributional capability after backbone adaptation. Increasing the flow weight to β=1.0 further improves deterministic decoding to PM = 1.073, driven by large CCC-V gain (0.387 vs. 0.308 for β=0.5) at modest cost to CCC-A and PAU . After per-AU calibration, this configuration reaches PM = 1.123, surpassing the calibrated plain fine-tuning baseline of 1.101; post-hoc expression calibration (Section 4.7) further raises this to PM = 1.177. Flow decoding for β=1.0 (PM = 0.931) trails the β=0.5 flow variant (0.956), suggesting that the stronger flow objective reshapes the learned target 12 S.E. Bekhouche et al. In both variants, deterministic decoding outperforms flow decoding after retuning, indicating that further alignment of the flow head with the adapted backbone remains an open direction. Kitchen-sink fine-tuning (Appendix H) applies class-weighted cross-entropy and per-AU positive weighting jointly during backbone fine-tuning. The combination does not improve PM over plain fine-tuning: PM = 1.041 vs. 1.045, with the AU gain (+0.042) offset by PV drop (0.047). Replacing ViT-S with ViT-B improves PV (0.354 vs. 0.325) but degrades PEXP (0.255 vs. 0.296), yielding PM = 1.116 calibrated; horizontal-flip test-time augmentation reduces all scores to PM = 1.111 calibrated, full results in Appendix D.

sections.title: 4.7 Per-Class Expression Threshold Calibration
sections.content: Section 4.4 showed that per-AU threshold calibration yields consistent +0.06 PAU gain by finding per-AU decision boundaries that maximise F1 on the validation set. We apply the same post-hoc principle to the expression head: per-class logit weights are tuned on the validation set to maximise macro-F1, directly addressing the systematic under-prediction of minority classes (Fear, Sadness) caused by the 8 expression imbalance. The full per-class breakdown is in Appendix (Table 26, Figure 3). Fear benefits most (0.038 0.331, +0.293), followed by Sadness (0.171 0.282, +0.111) and Disgust (0.465 0.507, +0.042); majority classes (Happiness, Other) are essentially unchanged. The large Fear gain confirms that the model has learned genuine discriminative signal for the rarest class: the default argmax simply never predicts it because its logit magnitude is suppressed relative to Neutral and Other by the imbalanced training distribution. Calibration recovers this latent signal without any retraining cost, in direct analogy to how per-AU threshold calibration recovers rare-AU signal (AU15, AU23) in Section 4.4. sections.title: 4.8 Summary of Ablation Results
sections.content: Table 5 collects the best representative result per configuration (all models use frozen DINOv3 unless noted; full 18-configuration table in Appendix A). Both training objectives are needed: flow-only (PM = 0.773) and det-only-withflow-decode (0.402) both underperform jointly-trained AffectFlow (0.826), and patch soft-pool further lifts the frozen-backbone deterministic result to 0.859. The best frozen-backbone result without calibration is 0.890 (AU positive weighting combined with class-weighted cross-entropy; Appendix G), while per-AU calibration alone raises AffectFlow flow to 0.888. Backbone fine-tuning is the dominant lever, lifting the best result to 1.045 (1.101 calibrated); flow retuning (β=1.0) on top of it reaches 1.073 (1.123 calibrated), and post-hoc expression calibration adds further +0.054 for the best overall result PMTL = 1.177. Kitchen-sink fine-tuning (Appendix H) and all expression/AU reweighting ablations (Appendices E, G) do not compound with backbone fine-tuning.

sections.title: 4.9 Discussion and Open Challenges
sections.content: All results in this paper are on the local s-Aff-Wild2 validation split; the SOTA scores cited in Section 2 are from the 7th ABAW hidden test set and are not directly comparable. The current best validated result (PM = 1.177) substantially improves over the official baseline (PM = 0.45); Figure 2 in Appendix visualises the full progression across key configurations. Within our controlled 14 S.E. Bekhouche et al. ablation study the gap is most pronounced in PEXP (best uncalibrated: 0.296; best calibrated: 0.350); two principal factors account for it. First, expression prediction remains the most challenging task: the per-class breakdown  (Table 24)  reveals that Fear and Sadness F1 remain below 7% at the default threshold, with predictions collapsed into Other. The 8 expression imbalance is the proximate cause; class-weighted cross-entropy and balanced sampling partially close the gap (PEXP = 0.2390.251), while post-hoc expression calibration recovers the latent rare-class signal directly, raising Fear F1 from 0.038 to 0.331 and Sadness from 0.171 to 0.282 without retraining. Second, the flow head requires retuning after backbone fine-tuning to recover distributional prediction capability: reactivating the flow objective during fine-tuning is beneficial, with β=1.0 yielding the strongest deterministic result (PM = 1.073; 1.123 with per-AU calibration; 1.177 with combined calibration), though deterministic decoding continues to outperform flow decoding in all fine-tuned variants. This suggests the flow objective acts as structure-aware regularizer for z, enforcing joint affect structure that benefits the deterministic heads even when flow decoding is not used at inference.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduced AffectFlow-DINO, multi-task facial affect model that treats in-the-wild facial behavior as conditional distribution p(y x) rather than deterministic mapping, learned via rectified-flow head over the joint 22dimensional affect space and validated through 26 controlled ablations on s-AffWild2. Three lessons emerge. The flow and deterministic objectives are complementary: flow-only training collapses without the deterministic anchor, and deterministiconly inference discards distributional capability. Flow sampling benefits VA estimation most, the task most sensitive to continuous ambiguity, and saturates rapidly, consistent with rectified flows straight-line transport. Backbone adaptation is the dominant lever, but the flow head must be retrained jointly during fine-tuning, or the generative pathway degrades. broader implication is that severe class imbalance suppresses learned signals without erasing them: post-hoc calibration unlocks near-zero F1 for rare categories (Fear, Sadness, AU15, AU23) at zero cost, suggesting decision boundaries can surface rare-class signal already encoded in the representation. Closing the gap to SOTA likely requires temporal aggregation, more expressive fine-tuned decoding, and joint backbone-flow multi-task scaling from the start.

sections.title: E Additional Ablation Studies
sections.content: This appendix collects ablations that did not improve over the AffectFlow baseline: VA loss weight, global AU threshold sweep, expression-enhancement strategies, label smoothing, and global AU loss weight. E.1 VA Loss Weighting Arousal estimation is the weakest component across all runs. Table 12 tests whether increasing the VA loss weight λV {1, 2, 4} can improve the arousal CCC without sacrificing other tasks. Doubling the VA weight (λV = 2) yields the best PM by improving PEXP without harming PV A, suggesting minor positive cross-task regularization. At λV = 4 the stronger VA supervision improves arousal but reduces expression performance and the overall score, consistent with negative transfer at extreme task weight imbalances [31]. The gain at λV = 2 is marginal (+0.004 PM L) and not consistent across ablations; we retain the default λV = 1. E.2 Global AU Threshold Sweep The default AU decision threshold is 0.5. Because AU labels are multi-label and class-imbalanced, we additionally evaluate simple global threshold sweep on the AffectFlow checkpoint. Table 13 shows that single global threshold does not improve over the default value; lower thresholds increase false positives, while high threshold of 0.7 sharply reduces AU F1. Color encodes training stage: frozen backbone (light blue), fine-tuned (dark blue), fine-tuned with both calibrations (red). The dotted line marks the official baseline (PM = 0.450). Backbone fine-tuning is the largest single jump; post-hoc per-AU and per-class calibration provide further +0.132 at zero retraining cost. Because the cross-entropy loss is dominated by majority classes (Neutral, Happiness) regardless of the weight, scaling the loss up merely competes for capacity with the VA task without directing gradient towards minority classes. Focal Loss for Expression. We replace the masked cross-entropy for expression with focal loss [29] that down-weights easy majority-class samples via the modulating factor (1 pt)γ. Table 15 reports flow-decoding results for γ {1, 2} with all other settings identical to AffectFlow. Focal loss does not improve PEXP and reduces PV by approximately 0.016. We attribute this to the relatively small number of valid expression labels (90K training frames) combined with heavy class skew: the focal modulator suppresses gradient from majority classes without providing enough additional signal on rare classes to compensate for the lost VA supervision. Per-Task Separate Projection Heads. To reduce negative transfer through the shared 768 768 projection, we give each task head its own projection MLP while keeping shared projection for the flow network. Table 16 compares this design against the shared-projection AffectFlow model. Separate projections slightly reduce all three sub-scores, suggesting that the shared representation acts as useful regularizer rather than harmful bottleneck in the frozen-backbone regime. Focal Loss with Separate Projection Heads. Combining focal loss with separate projection heads yields the configuration in Table 17: focal loss (γ = 2) plus per-task projections. The consistent failure of these expression-enhancement strategies establishes that expression imbalance requires structural remedies (class-balanced sampling, positiveweighted losses) rather than loss reweighting or architectural changes alone. E.4 Label Smoothing Label smoothing distributes fraction ϵ of the one-hot probability mass uniformly across expression classes, providing mild regularization against overconfident predictions. Table 18 sweeps ϵ {0.05, 0.10, 0.20}. Mild smoothing (ϵ = 0.05) raises PEXP to 0.230 and CCC-V to 0.276, but the gain is small and diminishes rapidly: at ϵ = 0.20, PEXP falls below the unsmoothed baseline. Label smoothing alone is insufficient to close the expression imbalance gap. E.5 Global AU Loss Weight As an alternative to per-AU positive weighting, Table 19 sweeps the global AU loss weight λAU {0.5, 1.0, 2.0}. Neither reducing nor increasing λAU changes PM appreciably; both variants plateau at 0.819. Per-AU positive weighting (Section G.2 below) achieves larger PAU gain (+0.043) than any global weight setting, confirming that perclass reweighting is more effective than global loss scaling for AU detection.

sections.title: Positive Weighting
sections.content: G.1 Balanced Expression Sampling WeightedRandomSampler assigns each training frame sampling probability inversely proportional to its expression class count, rebalancing the effective class distribution without modifying the loss function. Table 21 compares the balanced sampler alone and the balanced sampler combined with class-weighted cross-entropy against the AffectFlow flow baseline. The balanced sampler raises PEXP to 0.251 (+0.039 over AffectFlow flow) and lifts PM to 0.864, the best frozen-backbone result for expression. Combining balanced sampling with class-weighted cross-entropy gives PEXP = 0.246 under deterministic decode but PV drops substantially (0.193 vs. 0.238), indicating that the combined strategy over-suppresses gradient on majority classes at the cost of valence-arousal prediction. G.2 BCE Positive Weighting for AUs We set each AUs BCE positive weight to nneg/npos computed from the training set  (Table 20)  . One variant applies positive weighting to the AU head alone; another further adds class-weighted expression cross-entropy. Table 22 reports deterministic predictions throughout: flow decoding degrades both runs (PM = 0.777 for AU positive weighting alone and 0.745 for the combined variant) because the reweighted loss shifts predicted probability distributions incompatibly with the rectified-flow sampler. AU positive weighting raises PAU from 0.384 to 0.427 (+0.043) at training time. Kitchen-Sink Fine-Tuning We test whether the most effective frozen-backbone interventions, class-weighted cross-entropy and per-AU BCE positive weighting, transfer additively to the finetuned setting. Both are applied jointly during the same low-LR fine-tuning run as the plain fine-tuning experiment (Section 4.3 of the main paper). Table 23 reports deterministic predictions with and without per-AU calibration. The kitchen-sink combination does not improve PM over plain fine-tuning without calibration (1.041 vs. 1.045), despite raising PAU from 0.441 to 0.483. The AU gain is offset by PV drop from 0.318 to 0.271: class-weighted crossentropy reduces gradient allocation for the VA task when the backbone is jointly optimized, consistent with the pattern seen when combining balanced sampling with class-weighted CE (Section G.1). These results indicate that once the backbone is fine-tuned, frozen-backbone class-reweighting strategies add little value and can harm PV A; flow retuning is more effective path. Per-Class and Per-AU F1 Breakdowns Table 24 reports the full per-class expression F1 for the AffectFlow and fine-tuned models referenced in Section 4.5 of the main paper, and Table 25 reports the perAU F1 scores before and after per-AU threshold calibration for the fine-tuned checkpoint. Table 26 and Figure 3 report the per-class expression calibration results from Section 4.7 of the main paper. AU15 and AU23, the two rarest AUs (positive rate 2.4% and 2.9%), have near-zero F1 at the default threshold but recover to 10.8% and 18.9% after perAU calibration, confirming that the model has learned discriminative signal that the imbalanced training objective systematically suppresses.
